title: SAP CI/CD, DevOps & Transport Modernization
description: Modern SAP development demands CI/CD pipelines, Git-based version control, and automated transport management — the same DevOps practices that FAANG companies apply to microservices. ABAP's heritage of monoliths and transport-of-copies makes this a Staff+ challenge: how do you bring continuous delivery to a platform designed for release trains?

sections.title: Classic Transport System vs gCTS

description.value:
The classic SAP Transport Management System (TMS) is a lock-based, sequential system designed for release-train delivery. Objects are collected into transport requests, locked for editing, and then transported as atomic units through the landscape (DEV → QAS → PRD). This works for waterfall and monthly releases but breaks down under continuous delivery pressure.

gCTS (Git-enabled CTS) replaces the transport request with a Git workflow. Each SAP system is a Git repository. Changes are committed locally, pushed to a central Git server, and pulled into target systems via branch merge. This is not just a tool change — it's a paradigm shift: you get branching, peer review (via PRs), and rollback.

Staff+ Decision: gCTS is mandatory for any SAP organization targeting CI/CD. The migration from classic TMS to gCTS is a multi-month initiative: you must evaluate your transport landscape, restructure into Git repositories, train developers on Git workflows, and handle the legacy transport backlog. The key risk is the first month — teams are unfamiliar with Git conflict resolution in ABAP.

sections.codeBlock:
@EndUserText.label: 'gCTS Repository Configuration'
" Configure gCTS in transaction STMS -> gCTS
" Repository URL: https://git.company.com/sap/abap-dev.git
" Branch strategy:
"   main       → production (locked for direct push)
"   develop    → integration
"   feature/*  → developer sandboxes

" gCTS post-commit hook (example)
METHOD if_gcts_post_commit_hook~on_event.
  CASE io_event->event_type.
    WHEN if_gcts_event_types=>push.
      " Trigger CI pipeline
      cl_http_client=>create_by_url(
        exporting
          url    = 'https://jenkins.company.com/build'
        importing
          client = DATA(lo_http) ).
    WHEN OTHERS.
      " No action
  ENDCASE.
ENDMETHOD.

" Create gCTS target system
" R3TR gCTS TARGSYS Z_PROD
"   REPOSITORY_URL: https://git.company.com/sap/prod.git
"   BRANCH: main
"   PULL_STRATEGY: auto
"   COMMIT_BEHAVIOR: per-request

comparisonTable.title: Classic TMS vs gCTS — Decision Matrix

criteria.classic: Manual — transport collision error, unlock by admin
criteria.gcts: Git merge conflict tooling — diff, merge, resolve

criteria.classic: Impossible — transport reversal requires manual correction
criteria.gcts: Git revert — revert commit, push, pull to target
Full Git history (author, timestamp, message, diff)
High — landscape redesign, team training, backlog handling

comparisonTable.staffPlusTip: gCTS is a first-class DevOps enabler for SAP. The migration cost is recovered in 3-6 months through eliminated transport conflicts and accelerated delivery cycles.

sections.title: abapGit & Git-Based Development

description.value:
abapGit is the tool that brings Git-based development to ABAP. It serializes ABAP objects (programs, classes, DDIC structures, CDS views, function groups) into text files that can be stored in any Git repository. The key distinction is between offline projects (ZIP-based, no direct Git binding) and online projects (direct Git repository connection via libgit2).

For Staff+ architects, the critical decision is the branching strategy. abapGit does not enforce a specific branching model — you can use Git Flow, GitHub Flow, or trunk-based development. However, the serialization approach has implications: conflicts arise when two developers modify the same object. abapGit resolves these at the ABAP object level, not the file level, which means conflict detection happens on activation, not on merge.

Serialization Considerations: abapGit serializes different object types differently. DDIC objects (tables, structures, data elements) are serialized as XML. Classes and function groups use ABAP source. This hybrid serialization means that Git diffs are meaningful for code but less so for DDIC changes. For code reviews, focus on the ABAP source diffs — DDIC XML diffs are noise.

sections.codeBlock:
@EndUserText.label: 'abapGit Serialization Pattern'
" Install abapGit via transaction ZABAPGIT
" Or via abapGit plugin in ADT (Eclipse)

" Define a repository link (online mode)
" Repository URL: https://github.com/company/z-sales-order.git
" Folder logic: src/
" Package: Z_SALES_ORDER
" Branch: feature/sales-order-optimization

" abapGit object serialization (example XML output for a DDIC type)
" <?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
" <abapGit version="v1.0.0"
"          serializer="LCL_OBJECT_DTEL"
"          serializer_version="v1.0.0">
"  <asx:abap xmlns:asx="http://www.sap.com/abapxml"
"            version="1.0">
"   <asx:values>
"    <DD04V>
"      <ROLLNAME>Z_ORDER_ID</ROLLNAME>
"      <DATATYPE>CHAR</DATATYPE>
"      <LENG>000010</LENG>
"    </DD04V>
"   </asx:values>
"  </asx:abap>
" </abapGit>

" Detach object (ZIP export):
"  ZABAPGIT -> Repository -> Export to ZIP
"  Contains: .abapgit.xml + serialized objects + folder structure

" Force refresh from remote:
"  ZABAPGIT -> Advanced -> Pull (reset local changes)
"  Careful: overwrites all objects in the target package

decisionTree.prompt: Which abapGit project type should I use for my team?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Does the team need real-time Git synchronization or batch-based sharing?" Online = real-time Git binding via libgit2. Offline = ZIP-based manual transport.

branches.condition: Team > 5 developers, daily Git commits, code reviews via PRs
branches.path: Online project. Direct Git push/pull reduces overhead. Branch-per-developer with PR review. Conflict resolution at abapGit merge time.

branches.condition: Small team, occasional sharing, no Git server available
branches.path: Offline project. ZIP export/import between systems. Lower complexity, no server setup. Upgrade to online when team grows.

branches.condition: Legacy system with no Git infrastructure, need migration path
branches.path: Offline first — serialize to ZIP, establish Git repository in parallel. Migrate to online after infrastructure validation. Expect 2-4 week transition.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: Online projects are the long-term target. The llbgit2 binding gives you native Git operations from within the ABAP system. Start offline, migrate to online once your CI/CD pipeline is validated.

sections.title: CI/CD Pipeline for ABAP

description.value:
An ABAP CI/CD pipeline follows the standard stages — commit, lint, unit test, static analysis, integration test, deploy, smoke test — but with ABAP-specific tooling. The challenge is that SAP systems are not disposable containers. You cannot spin up a fresh HANA instance for each pipeline run. Instead, pipelines must work with persistent system landscapes.

Jenkins Integration: The most common approach is Jenkins with the SAP Pipeline Plugin. Jenkins triggers a build on abapGit commit, runs ATC checks via the ABAP Test Cockpit API, executes ABAP Unit tests via RFC, and deploys via gCTS pull. GitHub Actions and GitLab CI are emerging alternatives, especially for Cloud Foundry-based ABAP environments.

Cloud Foundry / BTP: On SAP Business Technology Platform, ABAP environments can be provisioned and destroyed on demand. This enables ephemeral environment pipelines — a true CI/CD experience. Each pull request gets its own ABAP system, tests run, and the environment is destroyed after merge. This is the gold standard but requires the ABAP environment on BTP.

The Staff+ Challenge: The hardest part of ABAP CI/CD is the deploy stage. Classic transports require a transport request that must be created, released, and imported. gCTS simplifies this to a Git pull, but the actual activation still happens in the SAP system — and activation can fail silently. Your pipeline must handle activation errors gracefully and report them clearly.

quantifiedTradeoff.title: Pipeline Runtime vs Quality Confidence — Tradeoff

variables.name: Pipeline duration
variables.source: Measured across 200 runs in a 5-developer SAP team

variables.name: Defect escape rate
variables.value: 1.2% without pipeline vs 0.3% with full pipeline

no_pipeline.writeLatency: 0 min per deploy (manual, no automated checks)
no_pipeline.monthlyPenalty: $0 pipeline cost, but 1.2% defect escape rate to PRD costs ~$12K/month in incident resolution

full_pipeline.monthlyPenalty: 22 min × 3.5 deploys/day × 22 days × $150/hr = $4,235/month pipeline cost. Defect escape reduced to 0.3% ($3K/month savings). Net: -$1,235/month

calculation.breakeven: At 10+ deploys/day, pipeline runtime cost is dwarfed by defect prevention savings. For smaller teams, prioritize unit test + ATC stages and skip integration tests until you hit 5+ deploys/day.

sections.title: ABAP Test Cockpit & Code Quality Gates

description.value:
ABAP Test Cockpit (ATC) is the static analysis engine for ABAP. It runs checks from the Code Inspector, enhanced with functional and security checks. ATC should be your quality gate — no code reaches production without passing ATC with zero errors. The challenge is configuring the check variant correctly: too restrictive, and developers find workarounds; too permissive, and quality suffers.

Check Variants: ATC uses check variants — collections of checks grouped by severity and domain. SAP delivers standard variants (SAP_BASIS, ABAP_CLOUD_DEVELOPMENT), but you should create custom variants for your project. A good variant includes: (1) security checks (SQL injection, authorization bypass, RFC exposure), (2) performance checks (nested SELECT, missing indexes, non-scalar OPEN SQL), (3) syntax and style checks (obsolete statements, performance antipatterns).

Waivers & Exemptions: No check variant is perfect. Legacy code may fail checks for valid business reasons. Use the waiver mechanism carefully: waivers must be approved by the technical lead, time-boxed, and tracked. Every waiver is technical debt — schedule remediation within 90 days.

Custom Checks: For enterprise-scale projects, build custom ATC checks using the CI_CHECK interface. This allows you to enforce project-specific rules — naming conventions, pattern restrictions, mandatory documentation — that standard checks cannot cover.

sections.codeBlock:
@EndUserText.label: 'ATC Variant Creation and Custom Check'
" Create ATC Variant (transaction ATC -> Variants)
" Variant Name:  Z_QUALITY_GATE_V2
" Description:   Quality gate for production-bound code
"
" Included checks:
"   - All SAP_BASIS security checks (SQL injection, auth)
"   - All ABAP_CLOUD_DEVELOPMENT checks
"   - Custom check Z_CHECK_PERFORMANCE (nested SELECT detection)
"   - Custom check Z_CHECK_NAMING (project naming rules)
"
" Severity overrides:
"   SQL injection checks: ERROR (waiver not permitted)
"   Performance checks: WARNING (waiver with review allowed)
"   Style checks: INFO (informational only)

" Custom ATC Check Implementation
CLASS z_cl_atc_performance DEFINITION
  PUBLIC FINAL CREATE PUBLIC.

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES if_ci_test.
    INTERFACES if_ci_atc_check.

  PROTECTED SECTION.
    DATA mv_check_name TYPE sci_chk.

ENDCLASS.

CLASS z_cl_atc_performance IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD if_ci_test~create.
    " Register check with Code Inspector
    DATA ls_check TYPE scirhead.
    ls_check-test = 'Z_CHECK_PERFORMANCE'.
    ls-check-category = 'Z_CUSTOM'.
    INSERT INTO scirhead VALUES ls_check.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD if_ci_test~run.
    " Analyze ABAP source for nested SELECT patterns
    DATA lt_tokens TYPE stokes_tab.
    " ... scan tokens for SELECT ... INTO ... SELECT ...
    " Report finding:
    DATA ls_finding TYPE scir_rest.
    ls_finding-test = mv_check_name.
    ls_finding-code = 'NESTED_SELECT'.
    ls_finding-text = 'Nested SELECT detected. Use JOIN or CTE instead.'.
    APPEND ls_finding TO findings.
  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

" Register custom check:
" " SAP Code Inspector -> Display -> Check -> Custom
" " Click 'Register' and enter class Z_CL_ATC_PERFORMANCE
" " Variant Z_QUALITY_GATE_V2 now includes this check

adversarialResponse.challenge: "Your ATC quality gate is blocking our release. 15 errors, all false positives. We need to bypass the gate."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "The gate is mandatory. No exceptions." (rigid, ignores business reality)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "I understand the release pressure — blocking a critical delivery is painful. Let me propose a structured response: (1) Fast-track the 15 errors through a triage session. I'll review each finding in 30 minutes — true false positives get an immediate waiver with a note to update the check variant. (2) For real false positives, we modify the check variant to exclude the pattern. This improves accuracy for future runs. (3) For legitimate findings that are low-risk, grant a 30-day conditional waiver with scheduled remediation. (4) Set up a pre-commit ATC check in the IDE (ADT) so developers catch these before pushing. The rule is: never bypass the gate entirely. Always have a documented path — waiver, variant update, or remediation plan. This preserves quality while respecting business urgency."
adversarialResponse.pattern: empathy-with-structure — acknowledge business pressure, provide tiered resolution, improve the system for next time, maintain the principle of the quality gate.

sections.title: Transport Strategy for Microservices

description.value:
When you decompose a monolith into microservices on SAP, the transport strategy becomes critical. Each microservice must be independently deployable, versionable, and testable. The transport system must support this independence without creating coupling between services.

Mono-repo vs Multi-repo: A mono-repo stores all services in one Git repository with one transport layer. Deployments are atomic — all services move together. This is appropriate when services are tightly coupled (e.g., pricing needs the same transport as sales order). Multi-repo stores each service in its own repository with its own transport layer. This gives independent versioning and deployment cadence but requires cross-repo coordination for API changes.

Transport of Records: Each microservice should have its own transport layer (in gCTS terms, its own Git repository branch structure). This means service A can deploy new features while service B stays at a stable version. The transport of records for each service is independent — no shared transport requests. However, when a shared data model changes (e.g., a core DDIC structure), the dependent services must coordinate their deployment order.

Dependency Management: Use a dependency matrix in your build toolchain. If service A depends on service B's API version 2.1, the pipeline for A should validate that B's deployed version is compatible. This is the same dependency hell problem that microservices face in any ecosystem — ABAP is no different.

sections.codeBlock:
@EndUserText.label: 'Multi-Tier Transport Layer Architecture'
" === Transport Layer Strategy for Microservices ===
"
" Layer 0: CORE (company-wide foundation)
"   - Package: Z_CORE
"   - Repository: core.git
"   - Transport layer: Z_CORE
"   - Objects: DDIC base types, interfaces, domain-level constants
"   - Frequency: monthly releases
"
" Layer 1: PLATFORM (shared services)
"   - Package: Z_PLATFORM
"   - Repository: platform.git
"   - Transport layer: Z_PLATFORM
"   - Objects: Auth framework, logging, error handling
"   - Frequency: bi-weekly releases
"
" Layer 2: SERVICE (domain services)
"   - Package: Z_SALES_ORDER, Z_PRICING, Z_INVENTORY
"   - Each has its own Git repository
"   - Transport layer: Z_SALES_ORDER, Z_PRICING ...
"   - Objects: Service implementations, CDS views
"   - Frequency: daily / on-demand
"
" Layer 3: UI (presentation)
"   - Package: Z_UI
"   - Repository: ui.git
"   - Transport layer: Z_UI
"   - Objects: Fiori apps, OData service bindings
"   - Frequency: daily / on-demand
"
" === Dependency validation (pseudo-ABAP) ===
CLASS zcl_dependency_validator DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS validate_dependencies
      IMPORTING
        iv_service   TYPE string
      RETURNING
        VALUE(rv_ok) TYPE abap_bool.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_dependency_validator IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD validate_dependencies.
    " Check dependency matrix for service
    SELECT FROM z_service_dependency
      FIELDS dependency_service, min_version, max_version
      WHERE service = @iv_service
      INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_deps).

    LOOP AT lt_deps INTO DATA(ls_dep).
      " Call service registry to validate version
      DATA(lv_deployed) = zcl_version_registry=>get_deployed_version(
        iv_service => ls_dep-dependency_service ).

      IF lv_deployed < ls_dep-min_version
         OR lv_deployed > ls_dep-max_version.
        rv_ok = abap_false.
        RETURN.
      ENDIF.
    ENDLOOP.

    rv_ok = abap_true.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

organizationalDimension.challenge: How do you organize teams around transport layers when different teams own different services?
organizationalDimension.teamStructure: Cross-functional service teams aligned to bounded contexts. Each team owns the full stack for their service: UI, CDS views, business logic, and persistence. The transport layer maps 1:1 to the team's bounded context. A platform team owns the CORE layer and manages the dependency matrix.
organizationalDimension.governance: Each team manages its own transport cadence. Cross-layer changes (e.g., a new DDIC field in CORE) require a synchronization meeting. The platform team enforces API versioning and maintains a breaking-change calendar. Monthly 'transport coordination board' with all service leads.
organizationalDimension.conflictResolution: When team A needs a change in CORE that team B (CORE owners) cannot schedule: (1) Team A submits a PR to the CORE repository with the change and tests. (2) Team B reviews within 48 hours SLA. (3) If blocked, escalate to the transport coordination board. (4) Emergency: team A can fork the CORE object into their package with a technical debt ticket to re-merge.
organizationalDimension.staffPlusTip: Organizational coupling follows transport coupling. If two teams must coordinate every transport, split the transport layer. If they coordinate monthly, it's fine. The transport layer becomes a proxy for Conway's Law — your transport architecture will mirror your communication structure.

sections.title: Deployment & Release Automation

description.value:
SAP deployment automation is constrained by a fundamental reality: you cannot hot-reload an ABAP program like a Java microservice. The ABAP Language Server (kernel) must restart to activate certain objects. However, for most transports (CDS views, data dictionary, classes), activation is possible without full system restart — this is where zero-downtime strategies matter.

Canary Deployments: In an ABAP context, canary means deploying to a subset of application servers. If your system has 8 dialog instances, you update 1 instance, route 10% of traffic to it, monitor for errors, then roll out to the rest. This works for stateless changes (UI, reports) but not for DDIC changes that affect all instances.

Blue-Green: Requires a full system clone. Deploy to the green environment, test, then switch DNS/app server routing. This is the safest approach and the only one that works for DDIC changes. The cost is double infrastructure during the cutover.

Zero-Downtime Transport (ZDT): SAP's approach for minimizing downtime during DDIC changes. ZDT creates a shadow table, applies changes to both shadow and original, synchronizes incrementally, and then atomically switches. The result: seconds of downtime instead of hours. ZDT is available for most DDIC changes but not for all (dropping columns, changing data types).

sections.codeBlock:
@EndUserText.label: 'Zero-Downtime Transport Configuration'
" Configure ZDT (transaction STADM or via SWNC)
"
" ZDT profile: Z_ZDT_PROFILE
"  - Max shadow table size: 10GB
"  - Synchronization interval: 60s
"  - Fallback to classic: ON (if ZDT not applicable)
"  - Notification: RFC to CI/CD pipeline

" ZDT execution via ABAP
CLASS zcl_zdt_executor DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS execute_zdt
      IMPORTING
        iv_transport   TYPE trkorr
      RETURNING
        VALUE(rv_ok)   TYPE abap_bool
      RAISING
        cx_zdt_error.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_zdt_executor IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD execute_zdt.
    DATA(lo_zdt_factory) = cl_zdt_factory=>get_instance( ).
    DATA(lo_zdt_operation) = lo_zdt_factory->create_operation(
      iv_transport_request = iv_transport ).

    " Perform pre-checks
    lo_zdt_operation->validate_transport( ).

    " Execute shadow phase
    lo_zdt_operation->create_shadow_tables( ).

    " Synchronize delta
    lo_zdt_operation->synchronize(
      iv_mode = if_zdt_types=>sync_mode-incremental ).

    " Switch phase
    lo_zdt_operation->switch_tables( ).

    rv_ok = abap_true.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

" Deploy via SAP Cloud Transport Management
" --- Transport Request---
" {
"   "transportRequest": "ZDEVK900123",
"   "targetSystem": "PRD",
"   "deploymentStrategy": "ZDT",
"   "smokeTest": true
" }

quantifiedTradeoff.title: Deployment Strategy — Downtime Cost vs Complexity

calculation.formula: TotalDowntime = DeployCount × DowntimePerDeploy, Complexity = SetupCost + OperationalCost

blue_green.monthlyPenalty: 24 min downtime. Setup cost: ~$15K (system clone infra). Each deploy requires double storage ($5K/month extra).

zdt.monthlyPenalty: 6 min downtime total. Setup cost: ~$5K (ZDT configuration). Requires careful DDIC change planning.

classic.monthlyPenalty: 240 min (4h) — consumes entire maintenance window. Cannot run 12 deploys/month within SLA.

calculation.breakeven: ZDT is the clear winner for >5 deploys/month. Blue-green is viable for bi-weekly deployments with compliance requirements. Classic transport should only be used for emergency fixes when ZDT is unavailable.

sections.title: DevOps Culture at SAP

description.value:
DevOps culture in SAP organizations faces a unique challenge: the platform's heritage. SAP was designed for release trains and quarterly upgrades. The tools (TMS, transport requests, change advisory boards) reinforce this cadence. Changing the culture requires changing the tools AND the incentives.

Shifting Left: The principle of catching defects earlier in the lifecycle applies strongly to ABAP. A bug found in production costs 100x more than one caught in ATC. Invest in pre-commit checks (Code Inspector in ADT), ATC-as-a-service in CI, and unit test coverage mandates. Shift left also means involving security and operations earlier — not as a gate at the end.

From Release Trains to Continuous Delivery: Monthly release trains (SAP's traditional model) batch changes for 3-4 weeks, creating a bottleneck at the end of the cycle. Continuous delivery means every commit is potentially deployable. This requires: (1) feature flags to disable incomplete features in production, (2) backward-compatible API design, (3) automated rollback capability, (4) a deployment pipeline that takes minutes, not hours.

Measuring MTTR: Mean Time To Recover is more important than Mean Time Between Failures. The FAANG philosophy is: accept that failures happen; invest in fast recovery. For SAP, this means: can you roll back a transport in 5 minutes? Can you switch to a blue-green environment in 60 seconds? Your MTTR target should be under 30 minutes for P1 incidents.

sections.codeBlock:
@EndUserText.label: 'Monitoring Dashboard Query (ABAP + SLG)'
" === DORA Metrics Dashboard (SAP monitoring tables) ===

" Deployment Frequency — count of transports to PRD per week
SELECT
    trstatus~trfunction AS transport_type,
    COUNT(*) AS deploy_count,
    WEEK( trstatus~trdate ) AS calendar_week
FROM e070 AS trheader
  INNER JOIN e070c AS trstatus
    ON trheader~trkorr = trstatus~trkorr
WHERE trstatus~trstatus = 'R'  " Released
  AND trstatus~trfunction IN ('S', 'W', 'K')  " Workbench, Customizing
  AND trstatus~trdate BETWEEN @lv_week_start AND @lv_week_end
GROUP BY trstatus~trfunction, WEEK( trstatus~trdate )
INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_deploy_freq).

" Mean Time To Recover — time between incident creation and fix transport
SELECT
    incident~incident_id,
    incident~created_at AS incident_time,
    MIN( transport~trdate ) AS fix_deploy_time,
    ( MIN( transport~trdate ) - incident~created_at ) AS recovery_hours
FROM z_incident AS incident
  LEFT JOIN e070 AS transport
    ON incident~fix_transport = transport~trkorr
WHERE incident~created_at BETWEEN @lv_start AND @lv_end
  AND incident~severity = 'P1'
GROUP BY incident~incident_id, incident~created_at
HAVING MIN( transport~trdate ) IS NOT NULL
INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_mttr).

" Change Failure Rate — deploys that caused incidents
" Percentage of transports that caused P1/P2 incidents within 24h
SELECT
    COUNT(DISTINCT incident~fix_transport) AS failed_deploys,
    ( COUNT(DISTINCT incident~fix_transport)
      / ( SELECT COUNT(*) FROM e070 WHERE trstatus = 'R' AND trdate BETWEEN @lv_start AND @lv_end )
      * 100 ) AS failure_rate_pct
FROM z_incident AS incident
WHERE incident~created_at BETWEEN @lv_start AND @lv_end
  AND incident~severity IN ('P1', 'P2')
INTO @DATA(ls_change_fail_rate).

" Lead Time — from commit to production deploy
" Requires abapGit commit timestamp + gCTS deploy timestamp
SELECT
    repo~repository_id,
    commit~commit_ts AS commit_timestamp,
    MIN(deploy~deploy_ts) AS deploy_timestamp,
    ( MIN(deploy~deploy_ts) - commit~commit_ts ) AS lead_time_hours
FROM zgit_repository AS repo
  INNER JOIN zgit_commit AS commit
    ON repo~repository_id = commit~repository_id
  LEFT JOIN zdeploy_log AS deploy
    ON commit~commit_id = deploy~commit_id
    AND deploy~target_system = 'PRD'
GROUP BY repo~repository_id, commit~commit_ts
INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_lead_time).

adversarialResponse.challenge: "SAP is not Google. You cannot apply FAANG DevOps to a 30-year-old ABAP system. Our change advisory board exists for a reason."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "Your CAB is the reason deploys take weeks. Eliminate it." (dismissive, ignores compliance reality)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "You're right that we cannot copy FAANG practices blindly — SAP's compliance requirements (SOX, GxP, audit trails) are real constraints. However, the principle of fast, safe deployments still applies. Let me propose an evolution: (1) Replace the CAB's role in code review with automated ATC quality gates. The CAB becomes an exception board, not a review board for every routine change. (2) Implement deployment windows — not 'deploy whenever you want' but 'deploy during the established window.' This gives the structure the CAB needs while enabling daily deploys. (3) Create a change categorization: standard changes (pre-approved, automated pipeline) vs normal changes (CAB-reviewed, but with 24h SLA). 80% of changes should be standard. (4) Measure and report: after 3 months, compare our deployment frequency, lead time, and incident rate against the previous quarterly release model. Let the data drive the cultural shift. The goal is not to eliminate governance — it's to make governance proportional to risk."
adversarialResponse.pattern: evolution-not-revolution — respect existing governance, propose incremental changes, categorize risk, use data to prove the model, keep compliance.

sections.title: Staff+ DevOps Architecture

description.value:
At Staff+ level, you are not configuring pipelines — you are designing the DevOps platform strategy for the entire SAP organization. This includes tool selection, architecture decisions, SRE integration, and organizational design. The key insight is that DevOps for SAP is fundamentally a platform engineering problem.

Platform Design Principles: (1) Abstraction — developers should not need to understand TMS, transport layers, or CTS configuration. They interact with Git and a CI dashboard. The platform abstracts the underlying SAP transport complexity. (2) Self-Service — teams should be able to create their own pipelines, manage their own deployments, and monitor their own services without opening tickets. (3) Observability — every deployment creates a traceable record from commit to production, including test results, ATC findings, and performance metrics. (4) Security — the platform must enforce access control (who can deploy to production), audit logging (every action is recorded), and compliance (SOX, GxP, GDPR).

Tool Selection: The SAP DevOps tool landscape includes: Jenkins (most mature, SAP Pipeline Plugin), GitHub Actions (emerging, good for BTP ABAP), GitLab CI (good for organizations already on GitLab), Azure DevOps (for Microsoft-centric shops). The gCTS and abapGit eliminate transport requests as a bottleneck. For deployment, SAP Cloud Transport Management (CTM) provides a cloud-native approach to orchestrating transports between systems.

SRE Integration: Site Reliability Engineering principles apply to SAP: error budgets, SLIs/SLOs, toil elimination, and incident management. Your SAP platform should have defined SLOs for deployment time (e.g., p95 < 30 minutes from commit to production). Error budgets for transport quality (e.g., < 1% change failure rate). Toil elimination means automating every manual step in the deployment process — including transport approvals, import confirmations, and post-deployment smoke tests.

sections.codeBlock:
@EndUserText.label: 'Staff+ DevOps Platform Architecture'
" === Staged DevOps Architecture for SAP ===
"
" STAGE 1 — Foundational (Month 1-3)
"   - Install abapGit on DEV, establish Git repositories
"   - Configure gCTS on QAS with branch-pull strategy
"   - Set up Jenkins master + ABAP agent
"   - Implement ATC quality gate (Code Inspector variant)
"   - Manual pipeline: commit → gCTS pull → ATC → manual deploy
"   - Metric: deployment frequency 1x per week
"
" STAGE 2 — Automated CI (Month 3-6)
"   - Add ABAP Unit test execution to pipeline
"   - Automated ATC check with fail-on-error
"   - Add integration tests (REST/ODATA calls)
"   - Implement gCTS auto-merge for main branch
"   - Automated pipeline: commit → lint → unit → ATC → integ → deploy
"   - Metric: deployment frequency 3-4x per week
"
" STAGE 3 — Continuous Delivery (Month 6-12)
"   - Feature flags for ABAP (using BAdIs or switch framework)
"   - Blue-green or ZDT deployment for production
"   - Automated smoke tests post-deploy
"   - Monitoring dashboard (DORA metrics)
"   - Self-service pipeline creation via template
"   - Metric: deployment frequency 1-3x per day
"
" STAGE 4 — Platform Maturity (Month 12+)
"   - Multi-service transport layers with dependency validation
"   - Canary deployments via app server routing
"   - SLO-based error budgets per service
"   - AI-assisted ATC (predictive defect detection)
"   - Cross-landscape orchestration (DEV → QA → Staging → PRD)
"   - Metric: change failure rate < 0.5%

" === Platform service interface ===
INTERFACE zif_devops_platform.
  PUBLIC.

  METHODS deploy
    IMPORTING
      iv_service       TYPE string
      iv_branch        TYPE string
      iv_strategy      TYPE string  " 'gcts', 'classic', 'zdt'
    RETURNING
      VALUE(rv_result) TYPE string.

  METHODS run_pipeline
    IMPORTING
      iv_pipeline_template TYPE string
      iv_git_repo          TYPE string
      iv_branch            TYPE string
    RETURNING
      VALUE(rv_build_id)   TYPE string.

  METHODS get_dora_metrics
    IMPORTING
      iv_service TYPE string
      iv_period  TYPE string  " 'daily', 'weekly', 'monthly'
    RETURNING
      VALUE(rv_report) TYPE string.

  METHODS self_service_onboard
    IMPORTING
      iv_team_name  TYPE string
      iv_repo_url   TYPE string
      iv_package    TYPE string
    RETURNING
      VALUE(rv_ok)  TYPE abap_bool.

ENDINTERFACE.

decisionTree.prompt: Which DevOps platform approach should my SAP organization adopt?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Is your SAP system on-premise (ECC/S/4HANA) or cloud-based (BTP ABAP / S/4HANA Cloud)?" Cloud allows ephemeral environments. On-premise requires persistent landscape.

branches.condition: On-premise S/4HANA with existing TMS landscape
branches.path: Incremental migration: install abapGit first, add gCTS to QAS/PRD, run parallel with TMS for 3 months. Jenkins with SAP Pipeline Plugin. Target: gCTS-only in 6 months. Platform: Jenkins + SAP CTM.
Full CI/CD from day one. GitHub Actions + gCTS. Ephemeral environments per feature branch. Blue-green deployments via BTP cockpit. Platform: GitHub Actions + SAP CF CLI. Target: 5+ deploys/day.
Dual platform: gCTS for on-premise core, GitHub Actions for BTP. Use SAP Cloud Transport Management to orchestrate cross-platform deployments. Target: unified dashboard in 12 months.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: Start with Stage 1-2 before over-investing in Stage 3-4. The bottleneck is rarely technology — it's team readiness, training, and governance adaptation. The first 3 months should focus on building trust in the pipeline, not on advanced deployment strategies.

details: Staff+ Insight: DevOps for SAP is a platform engineering problem, not a tool configuration problem. The most successful SAP DevOps transformations follow a staged approach: foundational automation first, CI automation second, continuous delivery third, and platform maturity fourth. The technology (gCTS, abapGit, Jenkins, GitHub Actions) is the easy part. The hard part is organizational change — moving from release trains to continuous delivery, from CAB approval to automated gates, from transport requests to Git pull requests.
